text: The surgeon performs surgical lysis of adhesions, mobilizing the small intestine and right colon remnant. A small window is created in the mesentery at the proximal and distal resection margins, followed by division of the mesentery using a tissue sealing device close to the intestinal wall to preserve vascularization and innervation.
Wet towels are placed over the exposed intestines to prevent fluid and heat loss during the procedure.

The surgeon marks the resection points and the anti-mesenteric wall by drawing a line on the intestinal wall. The bowel is then divided transversely using a GIA stapler placed perpendicular to the mesentery, ensuring the mesentery is positioned centrally between the staple lines. On the colon side of the anastomosis, the anti-mesenteric wall is situated between the two tinea coli.

The surgeon imbricates and reinforces the corners of the two staple lines using absorbable Lambert sutures. The two intestinal segments are approximated by tying together the corresponding corner sutures.

The surgeon sews the two staple lines together using interrupted trio or foro absorbable sutures, spaced apart to form a supporting column. This technique ensures a stable approximation of the two intestinal segments for the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an antimesenteric longitudinal enterotomy on the small bowel stump and a colostomy on the colon stump, creating a transverse lumen measuring approximately seven centimeters on the small bowel and eight centimeters on the colon. The incisions are made close to the supporting column, maintaining a distance of no more than one centimeter and no less than half a centimeter.

The surgeon performs the anastomosis by closing the longitudinal opening transversely in a single-layer gambi manner using trio or foro absorbable running sutures. The suturing begins with two sutures in the middle on the posterior or mesenteric aspect and progresses to join them on the anterior or anti-mesenteric aspect.

The surgeon observes the minimal mesenteric defect due to the close division of the mesentery to the intestinal wall. The anastomosis is covered with omentum to prevent adhesions at the anastomotic site.
